docs.title: PalmBee
docs.text: PalmBee is a package designed to automate the pollination process in palm tree plantations using drones. This package leverages the capabilities of Ardupilot and ROS2 Humble to provide an efficient and reliable solution for pollination.

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
• Ubuntu 22.04
  • ROS2 Humble
  • ZED SDK 4.2 ( with ZED Wrapper 4.2.5 )
  • Ardupilot
  • Jetpack 6.2.x

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Autonomous drone pollination
  • ROS2 + Ardupilot integration
  • No-GPS / Full Vision Flight

It leverages: - Ardupilot - ROS2 Humble - Jetson Orin Nano - Zed2i Camera

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
This section lists the main hardware components used in the PalmBee drone system.

The hardware setup is designed to support autonomous pollination using PX4 flight control, ROS2 processing, and vision-based navigation.

For wiring and connection details, see the Wiring Diagram page.

docs.title: System Hardware Overview
docs.text:
The PalmBee platform consists of the following core components:

  • Flight Controller
  • Companion Computer
  • Vision Sensor
  • Telemetry System
  • Optical Flow Sensor
  • Radio Receiver
  • ESC and Motors
  • Power System

docs.title: Flight Controller
docs.text:
Pixhawk 6C

The Pixhawk 6C is the main flight controller responsible for stabilization, motor control, and flight state estimation.

Key Features

  • PX4 firmware compatible
  • Multiple UART telemetry ports
  • Dedicated PWM outputs for ESC control
  • Supports MAVLink communication with companion computers

Role in System

  • Flight stabilization
  • Motor control
  • Sensor integration
  • MAVLink communication with Jetson companion computer

docs.title: Companion Computer
docs.text:
NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano

The Jetson Orin Nano acts as the companion computer responsible for high-level processing tasks such as computer vision and autonomous navigation.

Key Features

  • GPU acceleration for AI workloads
  • Runs Ubuntu + ROS2 Humble
  • High-speed USB interfaces
  • Supports camera and sensor processing

Role in System

  • Vision processing
  • High-level navigation
  • AI-based decision making
  • MAVLink communication with PX4

docs.title: Vision Sensor
docs.text:
ZED2i Stereo Camera

The ZED2i camera provides stereo vision capabilities for depth perception and environmental awareness.

Key Features

  • Stereo depth sensing
  • High-resolution RGB imaging
  • GPU accelerated processing via SDK
  • ROS2 integration support

Role in System

  • Depth estimation
  • Obstacle detection
  • Environmental perception
  • Visual navigation support

docs.title: Telemetry System
docs.text:
CUAV PW-Link

The telemetry module provides long-range communication between the drone and the ground control station.

Key Features

  • Long range telemetry link
  • MAVLink compatible
  • Reliable communication channel

Role in System

  • Ground station communication
  • Telemetry data transmission
  • Remote monitoring

docs.title: Optical Flow Sensor
docs.text:
MTF-01P Optical Flow

The optical flow sensor helps estimate ground-relative movement of the drone.

Key Features

  • Motion estimation
  • Altitude and velocity support
  • PX4 compatible

Role in System

  • Position estimation
  • Velocity stabilization
  • GPS-denied flight assistance

docs.title: RC Receiver
docs.text:
SBUS Receiver

The RC receiver allows manual pilot input when autonomous control is disabled.

Key Features

  • SBUS protocol
  • Low latency control signal
  • Compatible with Pixhawk controllers

Role in System

  • Manual override
  • Safety control
  • Flight testing

docs.title: ESC and Motors
docs.text:
Brushless Motors with ESC

The drone propulsion system consists of four brushless motors controlled by Electronic Speed Controllers (ESC).

Key Features

  • PWM control from Pixhawk
  • High efficiency propulsion
  • Standard quadcopter X configuration

Role in System

  • Thrust generation
  • Drone maneuvering
  • Flight stabilization

docs.title: Power System
docs.text:
The drone is powered using a 4S LiPo battery pack supplying power to:

  • Pixhawk flight controller
  • ESC and motors
  • Companion computer via power module

Power Distribution

  • Battery → Power Module
  • Power Module → Pixhawk
  • Battery → ESC Power Distribution
  • Regulated output → Companion computer

docs.title: Hardware Summary
docs.text: Component Model Flight Controller Pixhawk 6C Companion Computer Jetson Orin Nano Camera ZED2i Stereo Camera Optical Flow MTF-01P Telemetry CUAV PW-Link Receiver SBUS RC Receiver Motors Brushless Motors ESC PWM ESC Battery 4S LiPo

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: To install the PalmBee package, follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Install and setup ROS2 Humble First:
docs.text: using this Ros2 Humble documentation Ros2 Humble Documentation

docs.title: 2. Install and setup this specific Zed SDK:
docs.text: Zed2i SDK 4.2 note : this repository is based on wrapper 4.2.5 and tested on ZED SDK for JetPack 6.1 and 6.2 (L4T 36.4)

docs.title: 3. Create the workspace directory:
docs.text:
mkdir -p palmbee_project
cd palmbee_project

docs.title: 4. Clone the repository:
docs.text: git clone https://github.com/BeehiveCNG/PalmBee

docs.title: 5. Extract the 3 Zip File ( it's already in workspace form )
docs.text:
unzip palmbee_ws.zip -d palmbee_ws
unzip script.zip -d script
unzip yolo_ws.zip -d yolo_ws

docs.title: a. palmbee
docs.text:
cd palmbee_ws
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y

docs.title: b. yolo
docs.text:
before rosdep install / colcon build, please set yolo first here's the refrence to set it up : Yolo Setup

cd yolo_ws
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y

docs.title: a. palmbee
docs.text:
colcon build
or build specific package (for example, pb_perception) using
colcon build --packages-select pb_perception

docs.title: b. yolo
docs.text:
make sure the terminal / bash path are located in yolo_ws

colcon build

Note : there's will be some package that are missing and the ros2 build will encounter error if it's a clean install, please read the error message to know what package are missing and install it ( example : python3. . . or some other package like that)

docs.title: 8. Source the setup file:
docs.text:
source install/setup.bash

or set it up on ~/.bashrc using the source command

note : for yolo_ws / u can also build it on different workspace and build it too ( but u must install / set yolo first and any package that yolo needed )

docs.title: YOLO Setup (Jetson)
docs.text:
This guide explains how to install YOLO on NVIDIA Jetson using the provided installation package. The installation process is fully script-driven, ensuring reproducibility and minimal manual configuration.

Estimated time: ~10–20 minutes Disk usage: ~1–2 GB

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before proceeding, ensure you have completed the main installation:

👉 https://github.com/BeehiveCNG/palmbee-docs/blob/main/docs/setup/installation.md

docs.title: System Requirement
docs.text:
This setup is tested on:

  • JetPack 6.x (L4T r36.x)
  • CUDA 12.x
  • Jetson Orin series

Using JetPack 5.x or other versions may result in incompatibility with PyTorch and CUDA.

docs.title: 1. Extract YOLO Installation Package
docs.text:
Place the file:

yolo_installation.zip

Then extract it:

unzip yolo_installation.zip

docs.title: 2. Navigate to Installation Folder
docs.text: cd "Yolo Installation"

docs.title: 3. Run Setup Script
docs.text:
Make the script executable:

chmod +x setup_yolo_jetson.sh

Run the installation:

./setup_yolo_jetson.sh

The script will automatically handle all dependencies, environment setup, and compatibility adjustments.

docs.title: Python Environment Note (Important)
docs.text:
The installation uses:

python3 -m pip

which installs packages into the system Python environment.

Ensure that ROS 2 uses the same Python environment (typically system Python on Jetson). Otherwise, you may encounter:

ModuleNotFoundError: ultralytics

even though the installation was successful.

docs.title: Dependency Handling (Managed by Script)
docs.text:
The installation script ensures a consistent and compatible environment:

  • NumPy (1.26.4) Selected for compatibility with ZED SDK and Jetson ecosystem.

  • PyTorch (Jetson build) Installed with GPU (CUDA) support enabled for optimal performance.

  • OpenCV (system version) OpenCV is already provided by Jetson via system libraries (apt). Installing opencv-python via pip may override this and break compatibility.

These dependencies are configured automatically by the script. Modifying them manually after installation may affect system stability or compatibility.

docs.title: Expected Output / Verification
docs.text:
If the installation is successful, you should see output similar to:

===== SYSTEM CHECK =====
Torch Version: 2.8.0
CUDA Available: True
GPU: Orin
NumPy Version: 1.26.4
OpenCV Version: 4.5.4

YOLO model loaded successfully

Key indicators of success:

  • CUDA Available: True → GPU acceleration is active
  • GPU: Orin → Jetson GPU detected correctly
  • YOLO model loads without error

docs.title: Known Warnings (Safe to Ignore)
docs.text:
These warnings are EXPECTED and SAFE:

  • pip dependency resolver warning
  • SciPy requires numpy <1.25 warning
  • ultralytics missing dependency warning (opencv)

Example:

UserWarning: A NumPy version >=1.17.3 and <1.25.0 is required
ERROR: pip's dependency resolver does not currently take into account...
WARNING: Skipping opencv-python as it is not installed.

Do NOT attempt to fix these unless you fully understand the dependency chain.

docs.title: Optional: YOLO Test
docs.text:
You may optionally test YOLO inference after installation.

This step is only for verification. The model file (e.g. yolo11n.pt) is not required if you plan to use your own model later.

docs.title: Common Pitfalls After Installation
docs.text:
After a successful setup, certain changes may introduce issues:

  • Upgrading NumPy manually
  • Installing OpenCV via pip
  • Reinstalling PyTorch from pip

These actions may override the compatible environment created by the script.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Runtime Issues
docs.text:
1. Torch import error (libcudss.so missing) → Caused by incorrect PyTorch build (not Jetson-compatible)

  2. GPU not detected → Wrong PyTorch build or CUDA mismatch

  3. ROS node cannot find ultralytics → Python environment mismatch between ROS and system Python

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The installation process is designed to be:

  • Fully automated (script-driven)
  • Reproducible across Jetson devices
  • Compatible with ZED SDK and GPU acceleration

Steps:

  1. Extract package
  2. Enter directory
  3. Run setup script
  4. Verify output

After completion, YOLO is ready for use with GPU acceleration on Jetson.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
1. Companion Computer (Jetson Orin Nano)
  2. Pixhawk Flight Controller
  3. Sensors
  4. ESC + Motors

docs.title: Communication Flow
docs.text: Jetson → MAVLink → Pixhawk → ESC → Motor

docs.title: 1. Companion Computer Communication
docs.text: Device Device Port Connected To Pixhawk / Jetson Port Description Jetson Orin Nano USB Pixhawk 6C USB Type-C MAVLink communication (companion computer) ZED2i Camera USB Jetson Orin Nano USB 3.0 Jetson Stereo camera for vision processing

docs.title: 2. Telemetry & Sensor
docs.text: Device Device Port Connected To Pixhawk Port Description CUAV PW-Link UART Pixhawk 6C TELEM1 Telemetry communication to ground station Optical Flow MTF-01P UART Pixhawk 6C TELEM2 Optical flow sensor for movement estimation

docs.title: 3. RC Receiver
docs.text: Device Receiver Port Connected To Pixhawk Port Description RC Receiver SBUS Out Pixhawk 6C SBUS Pilot control input

docs.title: 4. Motor & ESC (Standard X Configuration)
docs.text: Motor ESC Signal Pixhawk Port Description Motor 1 PWM Signal MAIN OUT 1 Front Right Motor 2 PWM Signal MAIN OUT 2 Rear Left Motor 3 PWM Signal MAIN OUT 3 Front Left Motor 4 PWM Signal MAIN OUT 4 Rear Right

docs.title: 5. System Architecture Summary
docs.text: Component Function Pixhawk 6C Main flight controller Jetson Orin Nano Companion computer for AI / vision ZED2i Stereo camera for visual perception MTF-01P Optical flow sensor for velocity estimation PW-Link Telemetry to Ground Control Station Receiver SBUS Manual control from pilot ESC + Brushless Drone motor actuator

docs.title: STARTING
docs.text: TO START AUTOMATION / OPERATION

docs.title: 1. Run the Stack Script
docs.text:
The stack script is a .sh file used to simplify launching multiple ROS2 packages simultaneously. In this system, it runs approximately 6 packages (including rosbag for logging).

Location:

~/script/start_pb_stack.sh

docs.title: Steps:
docs.text:
chmod +x ~/script/start_pb_stack.sh
~/script/start_pb_stack.sh

docs.title: Notes:
docs.text:
• Wait until all ROS2 terminal windows finish launching
  • Ensure the script has fully completed execution

  • Check Mission Planner / GCS:

  • Make sure there is no "Bad Vision" warning

docs.title: 2. Start YOLO Detection Package
docs.text:
The YOLO ROS2 node is responsible for object detection and coordinate publishing.

Source location:

~/yolo_ws/src/yolov11_ros/yolov11_ros/yolo_node.py

docs.title: Build the workspace:
docs.text:
cd ~/yolo_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

docs.title: Run the node:
docs.text: ros2 launch yolob11_ros yolo_v11_ros_launch.py

docs.title: Notes:
docs.text:
• Wait until:

  • No new logs appear in terminal OR

  • Detected object coordinates start appearing

  • If objects are not detected while stationary:

  • Detection may still work during drone movement

  • Be cautious:

  • YOLO continuously publishes detected object coordinates

  • This may affect control behavior if detection is unstable

docs.title: 3. Start Control Manager Package
docs.text:
The control manager handles vehicle movement logic based on commands and detection input.

Source location:

~/palmbee_ws/src/PalmBee/pb_control/src/cm_setpoint.cpp

docs.title: Build the workspace:
docs.text:
cd ~/palmbee_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

docs.title: Run the control node:
docs.text: ros2 launch pb_control cm_setpoint.launch.py

docs.title: Notes:
docs.text:
• Alternative executables are available in pb_control if needed

  • After launching:

  • Wait until terminal shows:

    waiting for arm * Arm the vehicle using Mission Planner / GCS

docs.title: Behavior After Arming:
docs.text:
• The drone will:

  • Take off automatically

  • Execute predefined motion (e.g., circular trajectory)
  • React to detected object coordinates

docs.title: Additional Reference
docs.text: For more detailed documentation, please reference ROS2 Humble documentation: Ros2 Humble Documentation or check on Mission Planner Documentation ( GCS that being used ) Mission Planner GCS Wiki

docs.title: Summary Flow
docs.text:
1. Run stack script
  2. Start YOLO detection
  3. Start control manager
  4. Arm via GCS
  5. Drone operates autonomously

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
TODO

To use the PalmBee package, follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Launch camera (for example using Realsense):
docs.text: ros2 launch pb_perception rs_slam_launch.py

docs.title: 2. Launch AprilTag Detector:
docs.text: ros2 launch pb_perception rs_apriltag_launch.py

docs.title: 3. Get annotated image and coordinates:
docs.text: ros2 run pb_perception get_markers.py

docs.title: 4. Open your favorite image viewer to display the anotated image:
docs.text:
ros2 run rqt_image_view rqt_image_view

and select the image topic to display.
